title: Critical role of phase-dependent properties in modeling photothermal sintering of LiCoO2 cathodes
authors: Yang Hu, Benoit Sklénard, Wouter Vels, Yaroslav E. Romanyuk, Vladyslav Turlo
abstract: Photothermal (photonic) sintering crystallizes as-deposited amorphous LiCoO2 (LCO) cathodes for solid-state thin-film batteries using millisecond, surface-localized heating. However, process design often relies on 1D models with phase-averaged, temperature-independent properties, which can mispredict peak temperatures and thermal damage margins. Here we develop a multiscale, data-driven framework that provides phase- and grain size-resolved thermophysical inputs for stoichiometric LCO. We train an Allegro neural network potential with near-ab initio accuracy, enabling Green-Kubo calculations of thermal conductivity for crystalline and amorphous phases. The low, weakly density-dependent conductivity of amorphous LCO motivates its use as an effective intergranular phase in a thin-interface model that reproduces observed grain-size-dependent thermal transport. Combined with measured wavelength-resolved optical properties in 1D multiphysics simulations, we show amorphous LCO absorbs more strongly and reaches higher peak temperatures than crystalline LCO; thus crystalline, constant-property models systematically overestimate safe operating windows.
summary: This paper addresses the inaccuracy of using averaged material properties to model the rapid crystallization of LiCoO2 cathodes during light-induced sintering. By using machine learning potentials and molecular dynamics, the authors show that the amorphous phase has much higher optical absorption and lower thermal conductivity than the crystalline phase. This leads to significantly higher peak temperatures than previously predicted. These findings are crucial for designing safe and efficient manufacturing processes for solid-state batteries.
main_problem: Current models for photothermal sintering of LiCoO2 cathodes use phase-averaged properties that fail to account for the significant changes in optical absorption and thermal transport during the amorphous-to-crystalline transition.
main_result: The study demonstrates that amorphous LCO absorbs more strongly and reaches much higher peak temperatures than crystalline LCO, meaning constant-property models systematically overestimate safe operating windows.
method: A multiscale framework combining an Allegro neural network potential, Green-Kubo molecular dynamics, and 1D multiphysics COMSOL simulations with wavelength-resolved optical measurements.
model_or_system: The system is LiCoO2 (LCO) thin-film cathodes on Aluminum substrates undergoing photothermal sintering, modeled using a thin-interface grain-boundary framework to capture grain-size-dependent thermal transport.
key_observables: Thermal conductivity, optical absorptance, reflectance, attenuation coefficient, and peak surface temperature.
important_parameters: Grain size (60 nm to 3.3 um), temperature (up to 1500 K), and wavelength-dependent optical response.
main_assumptions: The model treats amorphous and crystalline LCO as distinct, static phases rather than a continuous, evolving mixture; heat capacity is approximated using the Dulong-Petit limit.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the computational workflow; Figure 3 displays effective thermal conductivity vs. grain size; Figure 4 shows wavelength-dependent optical properties and simulated temperature profiles.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of inaccurate sintering models, details a multiscale workflow (DFT to NNP to MD), presents thermal and optical property calculations, applies these to multiphysics simulations, and discusses the implications for process design.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Detailed workflow of the study. (i) A foundational neural network potential, Matlantis [25, 26], is used for broad structure sampling of stoichiometric LiCoO2 (LCO) across crystalline and disordered states. A benchmark set of 999 representative configurations is then recomputed with DFT+U+vdW (PBE+U with van der Waals corrections) [27, 28, 29] to provide consistent reference energies, forces, and stresses. (ii) Using this dataset, an ab initio-accurate neural network potential (NNP) is trained within the Allegro framework [30] and validated against DFT and experimental benchmarks for crystalline LCO (structure, elasticity, and vibrational properties). (iii) The validated NNP...

Figure 2: Amorphous LCO generated at different densities exhibits negligible structural differences. (a) Enthalpy and (b) density during thermal cycling under 0%, 5%, and 10% applied uniform tensile strain. Each system is heated to 4300 K for 100 ps to induce complete amorphization, rapidly quenched to 300 K over 4 ps, and then equilibrated at 300 K in the isothermal-isobaric (NpT) ensemble for 1 ns to relax residual pressure. (c) Radial distribution functions g(r) of the resulting amorphous structures. All three show a pronounced first peak at the characteristic metal–oxygen bond distance, dominated by Co–O correlations from local CoO6 octahedra, with a weaker Li–O contribution at a...

Figure 3: Grain-size dependence of the thermal conductivity at 100, 200, 300, 1000, and 1500 K. Experi- mental data are taken from Refs. [18] (gray crosses), [15] (cyan crosses), [16] (magenta markers), and [17] (black crosses). For Ref. [15], the plotted value is the orientation-averaged conductivity, 1/3(2×κ∥+ κ⊥).

Figure 4: Multiphysics simulations, informed by measured optical and computed thermal properties, show that amorphous LCO films reach higher surface temperatures than crystalline films across the investigated spectrum. Measured wavelength-dependent (a) absorptance (A), (b) reflectance (R), and (c) attenuation coefficient (α) of amorphous and polycrystalline LCO films. The vertical dashed lines mark the wavelengths corresponding to the minimum and maximum differences in absorptance (∆A) between the two phases, as well as the maximum difference in reflectance (∆R).
